comment_text: Those were two very improved bots in Switchback and Terratops both in their rookie seasons really struggled to do much of anything when it came to dealing damage. Yet were hitting extremely hard in their fights dominating two proven veterans who usually make it to the top 32 in regular seasons of Battlebots.

I truly can't express how much I miss the show, still. I'm grateful that the Pro League editing is getting better... but, nonetheless, none of this is what BattleBots should feel like. It felt so real before, and now it feels so fake, like something in an animated movie. And that's not just the editing, but the production style as a whole. The editing definitely has a lot to do with it, but the formats, the fights, the budget, the quality- it's all so different, and I get that some of that is out of your hands. But truly, from a fan who quite literally has grown up watching BattleBots, I miss the show format, the show production, the show... dearly. And if I were BattleBots, that's what I'd be striving to chase, because that's the thing that built the audience, that brought the people, that actually made people care about the previously niche world of robot combat. And so in every way possible, having such a drastic departure with Pro League hurts... like losing a loved one, or at least having them drift far away from you. Going back and watching old episodes and old fights really is a stark comparison. Pro League is fine on its own, but for BattleBots, it's still not what I crave and know the true production of the show is. I really, truly hope that, after Pro League, BattleBots will return to it's original format in some form, even if that's not on Discovery.

And Discovery made a huge mistake in cancelling you.

I've been watching Battlebots for years, and it's been fun. I'm just sick of the meta. More vertical spinners. Yawn. A lot of the bots even look the same now. Something needs to change. I believe I still prefer the TV show way of editing compared to this. as a longtime fan of the show and all robot combat events across the globe.

I’ve gone back and watched some of the most recent destruct-a-thon. Episodes, Like the Terrortops with Orbition. Ep.
That was slightly over 40 minutes long and It was edited amazingly. There wasn’t no weird color filters over everything. No music during the matches. And most importantly the teams got all the screentime they deserved. 

It’s very difficult to enjoy something when the plot is spoiled in the first 10 seconds of the episode. (Talking about Valkyrie vs Terrortops.) Not to mention the previous episode completely spoiled Ribbot vs Switchback???

My key takeways.
1. Episode spoilers. Slight engagements between the robots is fine! But not shots that send one of the robots flying. And clearly taking functional damage. (I’m genuinely confused how some of these were considered okey to reveal..)

2. Color filtering. Maybe it’s just me but all the images and video from the cameras seems extremely high contrast. just like it’s brightening every color on the screen. It was extremely noticeable in the first episode when Golden fury and Deathroll fought. There wasn’t about 4 seconds during that fight where the colors were how they have always been. Then the camera angle swaps and everything is super bright again. Not sure why they are doing it, I’m definitely not a fan! As someone who is sensitive to bright things I prefer the natural light.

3. Episodes should be longer. I assume they are shorter because of the YouTube algorithm. And I understand that completely, maybe battlebots is looking for new fans I’m not sure. They just need to be full length episodes. I wanna assume they are shorter so that the YouTube algorithm picks them up and recommends the episodes, so then the sponsor gets media attention.

Those are my take aways! 

Also 13:05 Sounds like a pumpkin getting smashed or some kinda fruit haha

I'm not gonna pretend I had it all figured out, because I didn't. Manifesting felt like failing for years. I'd try, get nowhere, tell myself I'd get it next time, and then nothing again. It got to the point where I figured maybe some people just aren't wired for it and I was one of them. Then I took Vera Hart's archetype quiz, kind of as a last thing to try. And it hit something. My archetype just fit, in a way that made me go oh. No fireworks, no life suddenly fixed. But for the first time I had direction instead of just fumbling in the dark hoping something would stick. If you're struggling with it too, honestly, do the quiz. Its free anyway.

The sound design is step backwards. In previous seasons, every massive collision felt powerful. You could almost feel the impact through your speakers. Now many hits sound surprisingly weak, almost like two lightweight plastic robots bumping into each other. These are 250-pound machines throwing enormous amounts of kinetic energy at each other - they should sound like it.

I'm also not a fan of the commentary audio. It has an odd echo or studio effect that makes it feel disconnected from the arena. In the Discovery seasons, the commentary felt much more natural and immersive.

Finally, I don't think the background music during the fights adds anything. The sounds of the robots themselves- the spinning weapons, grinding metal, impacts, motors, and the crowd are already the perfect soundtrack. Those real sounds create tension far better than background music ever could.

That being said, I do want to thank the production team for listening to the community. I noticed that the editing already feels less aggressive than it did at the very beginning, and the reduced number of jump cuts has definitely made the fights easier to follow.

Pro league should be called "the broken weapon league". There already have been more broken weapons in the first few episodes than in the entire previous season, and interestingly enough, so far all of them had drums, which were supposedly made out of S7 or some other tool steel. though in case of switchback, it was maybe perhaps not as much poor material as ribbot or copperhead, but rather just pushing it too far. Ribbot seemed to have more robust drum design wise, but i think that the material was either not the right stuff, or really poorly heat treated, i suspect the wrong material more. though even the switchback drum broke maybe easier than it should, but i think that it was perhaps just not perfect heat treatment, but they probably had the correct material.
